title: US PCE Nowcast – Headline inflation will creep above 4%
gist: Oxford Economics' US PCE nowcast projects year-over-year headline PCE inflation reaching 4.1% (the highest since April 2023) and core PCE climbing to 3.4%, driven by energy prices, higher portfolio management fees, and a memory-chip shortage tied to AI infrastructure demand. Despite this, it argues the Fed may still cut rates given service disinflation and a stable labour market.

insights.claim: Oxford Economics projects year-over-year headline PCE inflation reaching 4.1%, the highest since April 2023, with core PCE climbing to 3.4%.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: A 4%+ headline print is a hawkish data surprise risk for rates and a test of the Fed's tolerance for above-target inflation.
insights.quote:
The year-over-year headline PCE
inflation is estimated to reach 4.1%, the highest since April 2023, while core
PCE inflation is expected to climb to 3.4%.
insights.locator: Headline inflation is projected to rise above 4%

insights.claim: Core PCE is expected to outpace core CPI this year due to differing weights in the two indices.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: The PCE-CPI gap matters because the Fed targets PCE; watching the wrong index could mislead rate expectations.
insights.quote:
core PCE
expected to outpace core CPI due to differing weights in the indices.
insights.locator:
The forecast
indicates that inflation will remain elevated

insights.claim: Headline inflation is being driven by rising energy prices and higher portfolio management fees.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Energy and financial-services fees as drivers point to volatile, market-sensitive components inflating the headline rather than broad demand.
insights.quote:
driven by increasing energy
prices and higher portfolio management fees.
insights.locator: Headline inflation is projected to rise above 4%

insights.claim: A shortage of computer memory chips, amid ongoing demand for AI infrastructure, will keep US core goods inflation elevated.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: AI-driven memory shortages are now feeding into consumer goods inflation, linking the AI capex boom directly to the CPI/PCE basket.
insights.quote:
a shortage of computer memory chips will keep core goods inflation
elevated amid ongoing demand for AI infrastructure.

insights.claim: Persistent high costs in fertilizers and transportation, influenced by geopolitical tensions, will likely elevate US food prices.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: Geopolitically-driven input costs keep food inflation sticky, a regressive pressure that complicates the disinflation narrative.
insights.quote:
persistent high costs in fertilizers and transportation,
influenced by geopolitical tensions, will likely elevate food prices.

insights.claim: Despite elevated inflation, the Federal Reserve may consider a rate cut due to disinflationary trends in services and a stable labour market.
insights.speaker: Keith Mason
insights.so_what: A possible cut into 4%+ headline inflation is a contrarian dovish signal; service disinflation and labour stability are the conditions to monitor.
insights.quote:
the Federal Reserve may consider a rate cut due to
disinflationary trends in services and a stable labour market.
